perturbation.original_text: VNE shall use its reasonable best efforts to provide Converge with an initial response to and status reports for all Problems reported by Converge, and to resolve all Problems identified, in accordance with the provisions of Exhibit B attached hereto; provided, however, that VNE shall have no further obligation to respond to or attempt to diagnose or resolve a Problem once it is determined not to be attributable to Errors. VNE will review all Problem Requests submitted by Converge at the Problem Severity Level indicated by Converge unless another Problem Severity Level is clearly warranted. In the event of a reasonable uncertainty, the parties will assume a higher Severity Level for a Problem until they have sufficient information to make a determination that a lower Severity Level is warranted.
perturbation.changed_text: VNE shall endeavor to provide Converge with a response to Problems reported by Converge, and to address Problems identified, as outlined in Exhibit B attached hereto; however, VNE's obligation to respond or resolve a Problem ceases if it appears unrelated to Errors. VNE will review Problem Requests submitted by Converge, potentially at the Severity Level indicated, unless an alternative Problem Severity Level is contemplated. In situations of uncertainty, the parties may consider a higher Severity Level.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by weakening the obligation to respond and resolve problems. 'Reasonable best efforts' is replaced with 'endeavor,' and 'in accordance with' is replaced with 'as outlined,' creating uncertainty about the required level of commitment. The language around severity levels becomes discretionary with terms like 'potentially' and 'may consider.'
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text has a clear obligation that a company needs to respond to all queries and the changed text does not have the same language. Pennsylvania law requires that duties in contracts must be performed in good faith, meaning that using phrases such as 'endeavor' and 'may consider' does not create a legal obligation that must be adhered to.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-106. Definitions: "Contract"; "Agreement"; "Contract for sale"; "Sale"; "Present sale"; "Conforming" to Contract; "Termination"; "Cancellation". | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-106. Definitions: "Contract"; "Agreement"; "Contract for sale"; "Sale"; "Present sale"; "Conforming" to Contract; "Termination"; "Cancellation". (1) In this Article unless the context otherwise requires " contract " and " agreement " are limited to those relating to the present or future sale of goods . " Contract for sale " includes both a present sale of goods and a contract to sell goods at a future time. A " sale " consists in the passing of title from the seller to the buyer for a price (Section 2-401 ). A " present sale " means a sale which is accomplished by the making of the contract. (2) Goods or conduct including any part of a performance are " conforming " or conform to the contract when they are in accordance with the obligations under the contract. (3) " Termination " occurs when either party pursuant to a power created by agreement or law puts an end to the contract otherwise than for its breach. On "termination" all obligations which are still executory on both sides are discharged but any right based on prior breach or performance survives. (4) " Cancellation " occurs when either party puts an end to the contract for breach by the other and its effect is the same as that of "termination" except that the cancelling party also retains any remedy for breach of the whole contract or any unperformed balance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligations of VNE to respond and resolve problems, using ambiguous language like 'endeavor' and 'may consider' instead of 'reasonable best efforts' and 'shall'. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania law requires that duties in contracts must be performed in good faith, meaning that using phrases such as 'endeavor' and 'may consider' does not create a legal obligation that must be adhered to. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conforming' as being 'in accordance with the obligations under the contract.' By making the obligations less clear and more discretionary, the changed text creates a potential legal contradiction, as it's unclear if VNE is truly obligated to do anything specific, especially when compared to the original obligation of 'reasonable best efforts.'

perturbation.original_text: Converge agrees to install in the recommend environments all Maintenance Updates within a reasonable time following the date they are provided by VNE, taking into consideration any testing and customization required by Converge (the parties expect that the time for such installation 5 generally will not exceed 90 days). Should Converge find one or more material deficiencies in any Maintenance Update, Converge shall promptly identify such deficiencies to VNE in reasonable detail.
perturbation.changed_text: Converge will aim to install Maintenance Updates within a suitable timeframe following their provision by VNE, considering testing and customization needs (it is anticipated that installation will ideally occur within 90 days). If Converge identifies deficiencies in a Maintenance Update, Converge will inform VNE.
perturbation.explanation: The obligation for Converge to install maintenance updates within a reasonable time is weakened by changing 'agrees to install' to 'will aim to install', making the action non-mandatory. The 90-day expectation becomes an 'ideal,' further reducing the commitment. Replacing 'shall promptly identify such deficiencies to VNE in reasonable detail' with 'will inform VNE' reduces the level of detail required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of contract under Pennsylvania law
perturbation.law_explanation: In Pennsylvania, contracts require specificity in obligations. The original text creates a clear expectation of installing updates and reporting issues in detail. The modified text makes both vague, potentially leading to a breach of contract claim if Converge does not install updates in a timely manner or provide sufficient detail on defects, as the obligation to do so is no longer clearly defined.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-703. Seller's Remedies in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-703. Seller's Remedies in General. Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance of goods or fails to make a payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect to a part or the whole, then with respect to any goods directly affected and, if the breach is of the whole contract (Section 2-612 ), then also with respect to the whole undelivered balance, the aggrieved seller may (a) withhold delivery of such goods ; (b) stop delivery by any bailee as hereafter provided (Section 2-705 ); (c) proceed under the next section respecting goods still unidentified to the contract ; (d) resell and recover damages as hereafter provided (Section 2-706 ); (e) recover damages for non-acceptance (Section 2-708 ) or in a proper case the price (Section 2-709 ); (f) cancel. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text created clear obligations for Converge to install updates and report deficiencies in detail. The modified text weakens these obligations by changing 'agrees to install' to 'will aim to install', making the action non-mandatory, and by replacing 'shall promptly identify such deficiencies to VNE in reasonable detail' with 'will inform VNE', reducing the level of detail required. The change undermines the original intent of the agreement, creating an ambiguous legal obligation, as the level of compliance expected from Converge becomes unclear and difficult to enforce. The first snippet highlights remedies for sellers when a buyer breaches or repudiates, and while not directly related, it underscores the importance of clear obligations in contracts to avoid disputes and the need for remedies when obligations are not met.

perturbation.original_text: VNE will transfer to Converge all Maintenance Updates, and any associated Documentation, by remote telecommunications from the VNE place of business, to a Converge computer located at a Converge place of business in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, if any. If any such Maintenance Update and/or associated Documentation cannot be delivered via remote telecommunications to a Converge computer located at a Converge place of business in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, such Maintenance Update and/or Documentation will be installed by VNE on a Converge computer located at a Converge place of business in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, if any. Converge will not obtain title or possession of any tangible personal property, including any storage media, as a result of the delivery of any Maintenance Update or associated Documentation under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: VNE may transfer Maintenance Updates and associated Documentation via remote telecommunications from VNE’s business location to a Converge computer in Massachusetts, if feasible. If remote delivery proves impractical, VNE might install the update on a Converge computer in Massachusetts, if applicable. Converge will not acquire ownership of any physical media through these updates.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications replace 'will transfer' with 'may transfer' and 'will be installed' with 'might install', making VNE's delivery and installation obligations discretionary. Replacing 'if any' with 'if feasible' adds further ambiguity about the conditions for these actions. The removal of the language specifying that a Converge computer be 'located at a Converge place of business' also adds uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law emphasizes clear contractual obligations, especially in commercial settings. By weakening VNE's obligation to deliver and potentially install updates, the modified text could be deemed unconscionable under Pennsylvania law if VNE fails to provide the agreed updates due to this ambiguity. The original text makes it clear that, if able, the updates must be delivered and installed, but the changed text reduces the legal obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens VNE's obligation to deliver and potentially install updates, as it replaces 'will transfer' with 'may transfer' and 'will be installed' with 'might install', making VNE's delivery and installation obligations discretionary. This contradicts the emphasis on clear contractual obligations in Pennsylvania law, particularly regarding commercial settings. The original text makes the delivery and installation of updates obligatory, whereas the modified text reduces this legal obligation. Per scraped law snippet 1, § 2-302 states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract if it finds the contract or any clause of the contract to be unconscionable at the time it was made.
